subject: Psychology
grade_subject_fit: Introductory Psychology 11/12 lesson focused on evidence, method, interpretation, and limits.
unit_context: Cycle A, Lesson 1. Students build shared vocabulary for evidence-based interpretation.
assignment_purpose: Students compare examples and make a careful interpretation from limited evidence.
final_evidence_target: A short written interpretation that defines psychology, separates internal experience from observable action, and explains why evidence and limits matter.

student_task_flow.label: Part 1
student_task_flow.evidence: Definition uses science, thought, feeling, and action.

student_task_flow.label: Part 2
student_task_flow.evidence: Comparison separates internal experience from observable action.

student_task_flow.label: Part 3
student_task_flow.evidence: Interpretation includes evidence, an alternative, and a limit.

success_criteria: I can define psychology as a science.
I can separate internal experience from observable action.
I can use evidence before interpretation.
differentiation_model: All students work toward the same interpretation goal. Support uses sentence frames and example sorting. Extension adds a second interpretation and a better method for checking it.
checkpoint_release_logic: Teacher checks Part 2 before students move to Part 3. Students need one internal example and one observable action example before revising their interpretation.
teacher_implementation_notes: Ask students to name the observation before the explanation.
Require one limit in the final response.
likely_misconceptions: One example is enough for a broad conclusion.
A testable prediction is the same as a theory.
A confident explanation does not need limits.
pacing_shape: Single 50-minute lesson: launch, vocabulary build, guided comparison, interpretation revision, exit response.
assessment_focus: Comparison of internal and observable examples

teacher_guide.title: Cycle A Lesson 1 Teacher Guide — What Is Psychology?
teacher_guide.overview: This lesson introduces psychology as a science of thought, feeling, and action. Students practise moving from observation to evidence to interpretation while keeping limits visible.
teacher_guide.essential_question: How can psychology help us explain human action more carefully?
teacher_guide.big_idea: A strong psychology explanation connects a claim to evidence, uses a reasonable method, and names what the evidence cannot prove yet.
teacher_guide.opening_frame: Tell students that this course will ask them to slow down explanations. The habit is: describe what was observed, identify possible evidence, consider a method, and name limits.
teacher_guide.frame_line: Keep these four words visible: claim, evidence, method, limits.
teacher_guide.assessment_focus: The exit response should define psychology, compare internal experience with observable action, and improve an interpretation by adding evidence and limits.
learning_goals: Students define psychology as a science of thought, feeling, and action.
Students compare internal experience examples with observable action examples.
Students explain why evidence is needed before interpretation.
Students revise a weak interpretation by adding evidence, alternatives, and limits.
materials: Board anchor: claim, evidence, method, limits
integrity_checks: Use fictional or general examples only.
Keep language focused on actions and evidence.
Do not let one observation become a broad conclusion.
Require a limit statement before accepting the exit response.
teacher_notes: If students jump straight to explanation, ask what they actually observed.
If students give only a definition, ask them to apply it to the comparison example.
If students give only one possible explanation, ask what else could fit the same evidence.
If students finish early, have them add a stronger method for collecting evidence.
If the room needs more structure, complete Part A together and release Parts B and C after a quick check.
When debriefing, praise cautious language and clear limits more than clever claims.
questions: What evidence would make the interpretation stronger?
What method could collect better evidence?
What does the evidence not show yet?
How could two different interpretations both fit the same observation?
look_fors: Student uses science language in the definition.
Student separates internal experience from observable action.
Student includes at least one limit.
Student avoids turning one example into a broad conclusion.

timing.activity: Launch with claim, evidence, method, limits.
Teach the definition and compare internal experience with observable action.
Students complete task sheet Parts A and B. Pause for a quick check.
Students revise an interpretation using evidence, alternatives, and limits.

matching_bank: Claim: what I think may be happening.
Evidence: what I can point to.
Method: how the evidence could be checked.
Limit: what the evidence does not show.
Alternative: another interpretation that could fit.

model.title: Teacher model
steps: Observation: a student completes the written task but skips the optional share-out.
Careful interpretation: the student may prefer written processing, may need more time, or may not yet feel ready to speak.
Evidence needed: patterns over time, student reflection, and task context.
Limit: one action does not prove a broad conclusion.

content.subtitle: The science of thought, feeling, and action.

content.scenario: A strong explanation starts with what we can observe and what evidence could support an interpretation.

content.model: Stronger explanations move in this order: claim, evidence, method, limits.
supports: Limits: What does this not prove?

prompts: Psychology is the scientific study of ___.
Name one internal example and one observable action example.
Write one limit for an interpretation.

task_sheet.title: What Is Psychology? — Student Task Sheet
task_sheet.purpose_line: Practise explaining action with evidence, method, and limits.
instructions: Include a limit before you finish.

tasks.label: Part A — Define the field
tasks.prompt: Write a clear definition of psychology using science, thought, feeling, and action.

tasks.label: Part B — Sort the examples
tasks.prompt: List one internal experience example and one observable action example from the class scenario.

tasks.label: Part C — Evidence before interpretation
tasks.prompt: What evidence would you need before making a stronger interpretation?

tasks.label: Part D — Improve the interpretation
tasks.prompt: Rewrite a weak interpretation so it includes evidence, at least one alternative, and one limit.

success_criteria: I separated internal experience from observable action.

exit_ticket.title: Exit Ticket — What Is Psychology?
exit_ticket.prompt: In 3-5 sentences, explain what psychology studies and why evidence and limits matter when interpreting human action.
